medications.indication_rationale: The mainstay of eczema care — restore the skin barrier; apply liberally and after showering.
Adequately potent topical steroid to the inflamed flexures (step down as it settles).
Night-time sedation/itch relief to restore sleep (short-term).
medications.dose_route_frequency: e.g., 25 mg at night (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: May be used cautiously for a severe refractory flare, but risks rebound; optimise topical therapy first and taper. (As per source note.)
contraindications_in_this_case: Rebound flare risk; avoid repeated courses

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Lukewarm (not hot) short showers, pat dry, and apply moisturiser within ~3 minutes; moisturise everywhere at least 4x/day; steroid ointment only to red/itchy areas.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Locks in moisture, repairs the barrier and reduces itch.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Reduce heating/humidify the air and manage the itch to protect sleep.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Dry heated air is a key trigger; sleep loss worsens distress.

patient_education_points.point: This is a severe eczema flare set off by the cold, dry air and your recent cold. Moisturiser is as important as the steroid cream — use lots of it, and the steroid on the red areas.
The sedating antihistamine (± short steroid course) should help you sleep and settle the flare over a few days.
If you develop clusters of small identical punched-out sores with fever, or the skin becomes hot, swollen and honey-crusted — seek urgent/emergency care (these are serious infections).

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Flare control and steroid tapering; establish long-term maintenance (emollients ± maintenance topical therapy).
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Punched-out sores + fever (herpetic), or honey crust/spreading hot skin + fever (bacterial) — urgent/emergency care.

must_include_items: Diagnose an atopic dermatitis flare and screen for eczema herpeticum and impetiginisation
Emollients (mainstay) + adequately potent topical corticosteroid + trigger control
Itch/sleep support to break the itch-scratch cycle
Safety-net for herpetic and bacterial complications
acceptable_alternatives: Alternative emollient/topical steroid potency by site
Sedating antihistamine at night; short oral steroid only for refractory severe flare
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires eczema fundamentals (emollient + topical steroid + triggers) AND an eczema-herpeticum/bacterial safety-net; missing eczema herpeticum is the key clinical failure.
